Areas of Expertise

A detailed view of my technical domains — built across 10+ years of research, industrial development, and international collaboration.

⚡ Hydrogen & Fuel Cells ⚙️ Compressors, Pumps & Valves 🌊 Fluid Mechanics 🤖 ML & Data Science 📋 Norms & Regulations
⚡

Hydrogen & Fuel Cell Systems

Industrial development, commissioning, and certification of a 500 kW modular PEM fuel cell and steam reformer system for maritime, bus, and truck applications.

At Freudenberg e-Power Systems, I was responsible for the development and commissioning of a 500 kW modular fuel cell system — one of the most powerful units developed for marine and heavy-duty transport. My role spanned the full engineering lifecycle from requirements definition through system certification.

I managed approximately 20 international suppliers, with a strategic focus on the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland) to ensure optimal communication quality, rapid response times, and close engineering collaboration. Supplier activities included specification reviews, design audits, qualification tests, and interface management.

I was responsible for the commissioning of the complete 500 kW system — including cold, warm, and hot commissioning phases — and specified individual commissioning steps myself based on system requirements and safety regulations.

Steam reformer for maritime: In parallel, I contributed to a maritime steam reformer project — a system that converts liquid hydrocarbons into hydrogen-rich gas for shipboard fuel cell operation, where direct hydrogen storage is impractical.

All components and subsystems were certified under Lloyd's Register and ABS (American Bureau of Shipping) — two of the most demanding maritime classification bodies globally.

PEM fuel cell operating principle — hydrogen is oxidized at the anode, oxygen reduced at the cathode, producing electricity, water, and heat
High-power fuel cell systems enable zero-emission operation in heavy transport — maritime vessels, buses, and trucks
500 kW PEM fuel cellSteam reformer (maritime) Full system commissioning~20 international suppliers (DACH focus) Component ownershipDeputy team lead Polarion ALM/RMFMEA Lloyd's RegisterABS IATF 16949IEC 61000ISO 9001 Marine · Bus · Truck

Compressors, Pumps, Valves & Injection Systems

Industrial component ownership of fluid machinery and deep-dive research into high-pressure GDI fuel injectors using synchrotron X-ray and high-speed optical methods.

At Freudenberg, I held technical ownership of all critical fluid and mechanical components in the 500 kW fuel cell system. I coordinated the full supplier interface — from specification and design review through qualification testing and series release.

Component portfolio: turbo compressors, centrifugal pumps, diaphragm pumps, on/off valves, safety relief valves, water separators, vent valves, process filters, and control/regulation valves. Commissioning included leak and pressure tests according to system and regulatory requirements.

My Bosch PhD added deep understanding of high-pressure injection nozzle physics. GDI injectors operate at up to 200 bar, with flow phenomena at micrometer scales and microsecond timescales — cavitation, hydraulic flip, string cavitation, and primary spray breakup.

Using transparent nozzles, high-speed shadowgraphy, micro-PIV, and synchrotron XPCI at Argonne National Laboratory, I produced the first experimental data linking transient needle dynamics directly to near-nozzle spray structure.

📖 Research publications — interactive figure browser

Cavitation probability (false color) and fluctuation maps for cylindrical and divergent nozzle holes. Injection into air vs. liquid environment — air entrainment found negligible during steady-state.
Effect of backpressure (1–7 bar) on cavitation in cylindrical holes. Higher backpressure increases cavitation fluctuation frequency and reduces cavitation extent.
Projected cavitation area over injection time for both nozzle holes (low and high inclination angle). Steady-state phase identified; FFT applied after third-order polynomial detrending.
Dominant cavitation fluctuation frequency vs. backpressure for all hole types. Divergent holes produce more stable (lower frequency) cavitation structures. Key finding: frequency scales strongly with backpressure.
ILASS 2017, Valencia — First high-speed shadowgraphy study of real-size two-hole GDI nozzles at 120,000 fps. The ambiguity between cavitation and air entrainment was resolved by injecting into liquid vs. gas environments. Backpressure dependence of cavitation frequency was quantified using FFT analysis.
Graphic abstract (Exp. Fluids 2020) — micro-PIV velocity field (left) and RANS-reconstructed static pressure distribution (right) inside real-scale GDI nozzle at 100 bar injection pressure.
String cavitation initiation sequence captured at 5 MHz (Δt = 0.2 μs between frames). Two cavitation origins merge — one from the hole interior, one near the needle — forming a continuous string extending to the hole exit.
Static pressure distribution (left) and contribution of RANS fluctuating terms (right). Two fluctuating regions identified — one linked to rotating flow (string cavitation precursor), one to the recirculation zone near hole inlet.
High-speed sequence showing string cavitation growth during needle closing phase at 50 bar. The cavitation rapidly extends from near-needle area to the full hole length.
Experiments in Fluids 2020 (Springer) — First micro-PIV measurement inside a real-scale GDI nozzle using 5 MHz ultra-high-speed imaging. Velocity data used for RANS-based pressure reconstruction and CFD validation (ANSYS CFX). The fluctuating RANS terms enabled qualitative prediction of string cavitation locations without direct cavitation visualization.
Schematic of the two aluminum nozzle types investigated at Argonne. Type A and B differ in hole inclination angles, distances, and counterbore geometry. Nozzles machined from aluminum for X-ray transparency.
Synchrotron XPCI image sequence over the complete injection event (Type A nozzle). Internal cavitation structure (highlighted) and near-nozzle jet structure visible simultaneously. Hydraulic flip during needle overshoot clearly identified.
XPCI images of the near-nozzle liquid jet from Type B nozzle during needle overshoot. Smooth and perturbed jet surface domains alternate — their distribution reveals the transient cavitation state inside the nozzle.
Quantitative transient jet parameters over injection time (Type A, 10 MPa). Jet boundary angles, characteristic width, jet direction, and expansion angle — all show clear transitions between overshoot and steady-state phases.
International Journal of Engine Research (IJER) — Experiments conducted at the 7ID beamline of Argonne National Laboratory (Advanced Photon Source). Synchrotron X-ray phase-contrast imaging (XPCI) at 67,889 fps with 150 ps X-ray pulses. First experimental evidence of hydraulic flip inside aluminum GDI nozzles linked to transient needle overshooting. Near-nozzle spray boundary tracking used to assess in-nozzle cavitation state.

🔬 Interactive: Backpressure effect on cavitation frequency

Based on data from Mamaikin et al. (ILASS 2017) — slide to explore how backpressure changes cavitation fluctuation frequency in GDI nozzle holes.

At 4 bar backpressure: cavitation fluctuation frequency ~25–35 kHz (cylindrical holes) · 15–20 kHz (divergent holes) · Cavitation layer is thinner → larger observable fluctuation area
Dominant cavitation fluctuation frequency vs. backpressure (Fig. 11 from Mamaikin et al. ILASS 2017). Cylindrical holes show higher frequencies; divergent holes produce more stable structures.

Fluid Mechanics

From nuclear boiling regimes to GDI spray dynamics — experimental and numerical expertise in complex two-phase and turbulent flows.

My fluid mechanics background spans the full spectrum from fundamental boiling physics (BSc — nucleate, transition, film boiling, Leidenfrost effect on superheated surfaces in nuclear power plant context) through spray combustion (MSc — laminar burning velocities using Heat Flux burner; LDA/PDA droplet measurements) to cavitating injector flows (PhD — micro-PIV, shadowgraphy, synchrotron XPCI).

During my PhD, I developed a novel approach combining 2D micro-PIV velocity data with RANS-based pressure reconstruction to obtain the first experimental pressure distribution inside a real-scale GDI nozzle. The velocity field revealed strongly asymmetric profiles, recirculation zones, and swirling structures that predict string cavitation onset.

Key methodological innovation: The fluctuating terms of the RANS equations — computed from time-resolved PIV velocity sequences — were used to identify rotating flow structures and qualitatively locate cavitation-prone regions inside the nozzle hole. This allowed 3D flow characterization from 2D experimental data.

At FAU, I led a sub-project on EV battery thermal management within a €3M collaborative program — applying heat transfer expertise to liquid cooling system development for high-power battery packs.

Mean velocity field (micro-PIV, 5 MHz) and RANS-reconstructed static pressure — published in Experiments in Fluids 2020. Pressure range 2–100 bar; velocity 10–130 m/s inside the nozzle hole.

🎬 Injection phase explorer — what happens inside the nozzle?

XPCI image during needle overshoot (Type A nozzle, 10 MPa). Cavitation covers both sides of hole — hydraulic flip condition. Emerging jet appears narrow and smooth.

Needle overshooting: During the opening transient, the needle briefly rises to nearly double its steady-state lift. This creates a nearly symmetric inflow into the nozzle hole from both the sac and needle-seat side simultaneously.

Under these conditions, cavitation can form on both sides of the hole inlet simultaneously — a phenomenon known as hydraulic flip. The fuel jet passes through the hole without direct wall contact, resulting in a narrow, smooth, low-divergence jet.

This was observed for the first time inside an aluminum GDI nozzle using synchrotron XPCI at Argonne.

Steady-state pressure distribution inside GDI nozzle (RANS reconstruction from micro-PIV data). Minimum pressure on inner side of injector — primary cavitation inception site.

Steady-state injection: At design needle lift, the dominant flow comes from the sac side. A high pressure drop occurs at the inner edge of the nozzle hole inlet, creating a low-pressure zone that drives partial supercavitation.

The velocity field shows strong asymmetry: higher velocities near the outer side, a recirculation region on the inner side. RANS fluctuating terms reveal two key instability zones corresponding to the observed string cavitation locations.

Velocity range measured: 10–130 m/s (expected max ~150 m/s per Bernoulli).

String cavitation initiation during needle closing phase (5 MHz, Δt = 0.2 μs). Two origins visible — merging into a continuous cavitation string spanning needle to hole exit.

Needle closing: As the needle descends, flow vorticity in the sac increases dramatically. This promotes string cavitation — vortex-core cavitation that appears as thread-like gas structures aligned with rotating flow.

The string cavitation was shown to initiate at two separate locations simultaneously — one in the lower central hole region, one near the needle — before they merge into a single continuous structure.

This sequence was captured at the unprecedented rate of 5 million frames per second using the ultra-high-speed Kirana camera with SILUX400 laser (10 ns pulses).

Two-phase flowCavitation (geometric, string, hydraulic flip) Spray dynamics & primary breakupRANS / URANS Pressure reconstructionANSYS CFX Heat transferBoiling regimes / Leidenfrost Laminar burning velocityLDA / PDA EV battery thermal managementMATLAB · Python
🤖

Machine Learning & Data Science

Active development of ML and AI competencies, building on a strong foundation in scientific data analysis, image processing, and experimental physics.

Throughout my research career I have worked extensively with large experimental datasets — PIV velocity fields with millions of vectors, time-resolved image sequences at up to 5 MHz, multi-channel sensor data from fuel cell test benches, and FFT-analyzed cavitation signals. Processing and interpreting this data in MATLAB and Python has been central to all my publications.

Specific data science methods applied in my research include: cross-correlation algorithms (PIV), FFT spectral analysis (cavitation frequency), RANS statistical decomposition, iterative pressure integration (Poisson solver), image binarization and entropy filtering (XPCI), and vortex model fitting (Gaussian/Lamb-Oseen).

Norms, Standards & Regulations

Practical experience with quality management, functional safety, electromagnetic compatibility, maritime classification, and project management standards.

Quality Management Systems — applied in engineering workflows, documentation, and supplier qualification at Freudenberg e-Power Systems.
IATF 16949
Automotive QMS — applied for component development and supplier qualification within the 500 kW fuel cell system program.
IEC 61000
Electromagnetic Compatibility — considered in system integration and component qualification for the fuel cell system.
Lloyd's Register
Maritime classification — led certification of fuel cell system components under Lloyd's Register rules for marine propulsion systems.
ABS (American Bureau of Shipping)
US maritime classification — parallel certification alongside Lloyd's Register for fuel cell components in marine applications.
